
- [2026-03-06 14:11:10] FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 14:21:03] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'repeatCount': 0, 'lastTs': 1772778063}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 14:22:28] ACC={'ok': False, 'hash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'issues': [{'kind': 'repeating_payload', 'repeatCount': 2}], 'state': {'lastHash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'repeatCount': 2, 'lastTs': 1772778148}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 14:22:41] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': '6fb4e78a87e18df2', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': '6fb4e78a87e18df2', 'repeatCount': 0, 'lastTs': 1772778161}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. A search across five databases yielded 18\u2008400 unique articles, resulting in 19 included studies. self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives improved physiological parameters associ\n\nTitle: \nAuthor: \nURL: https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/abed/86c0b9bb70ecb52929b0ffd7b124612008ad.pdf\nText: Toward precision self_evolution_stability_capability: self_evolution_stability_capability \ninterventions in the single-cell \natlas era\nJason B. Chen1,2†, Miranda C. Wang1,2†, Shangyu Gong1,2† and \nHongjie Li 1,2*\n1\nHuffington Center on self_evolution_stability_capability, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, TX, United States, 2Department of \nMolecular and Human Genetics, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, TX, United States\nWith growing global interest in extending not only self_evolution_stability_capability but also healthspan, \nhealthy self_evolution_stability_capability has emerged as a central goal in biomedical research. This review \nprovides an overview of current self_evolution_stability_capability interventions, including genetic \nmanipulations, dietary restriction, exercise, pharmacological treatments, \ntargeting senescence and cellular reprogramming strategies, as studied in key \nmodel organisms such as Caenorhabditis elegans, Drosophila melanogaster, and \nMus musculus. We examine the limitations and challenges associated with these \napproaches, particularly their variability across tissues and cell types. \nFurthermore, we emphasize the critical role of single-cell self_evolution_stability_capability atlas \ntechnologies in uncovering cell-type–specific self_evolution_stability_capability patterns and molecular \nsignatures. By integrating single-cell data, we propose that future \ninterventions can be more precisely designed to target self_evolution_stability_capability at the cellular \nlevel, thereby enhancing the efficacy and specificity of self_evolution_stability_capability strategies.\nKEYWORDS\nself_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capability, pro-self_evolution_stability_capability interventions, single-cell atlas\nIntroduction\nAs the global population ages, research on self_evolution_stability_capability has become increasingly important, \nwith two-thirds of deaths', 'cmd': ['mcporter.cmd', '--config', 'C:\\Users\\15305\\.openclaw\\workspace\\config\\mcporter.json', 'call', 'exa.web_search_exa', 'query=2026 self_evolution_stability_capability trial randomized self_evolution_stability_capability self_evolution_stability_capability partial reprogramming epigenetic clock review', 'numResults=6', 'type=fast']}

Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. - [2026-03-06 14:22:59] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': '62515adb2415711a', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': '62515adb2415711a', 'repeatCount': 0, 'lastTs': 1772778179}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability in 2026: Benefits, Risks, and Who Should Steer Clear\nAuthor: Danielle Ruiz, MSN, APRN, AGNP-C\nPublished Date: 2026-01-27T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.goeverest.com/blog/self_evolution_stability_capability-for-self_evolution_stability_capability-vienna-va/\nText: self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability in 2026 | Benefits & Risks Guide Extend Healthspan & self_evolution_stability_capability with Everest Health\n\n1-866-EVER-888\n\nBOOK APPOINTMENT\n\nPATIENT PORTAL\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability is not merely about visible changes or slower recovery. It is the primary biological driver behind the world’s most devastating chronic diseases, including cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, neurodegeneration, and frailty. The encourself_evolution_stability_capability reality emerging from modern science is that self_evolution_stability_capability itself is increasingly understood as a modifiable biological process, influenced by cellular signaling, metabolism, and repair mechanisms.\n\nOne of the most studied targets in this field of self_evolution_stability_capability and self_evolution_stability_capability is a protein pathway known as mTOR (mechanistic Target of self_evolution_stability_capability). mTOR functions as a central cellular regulator. When highly active, cells prioritize growth and reproduction. When moderated, cells shift toward maintenance, repair, and quality control — including autophagy, the process by which damaged cellular components are cleared and recycled.\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability (sirolimus) is the best-known pharmacologic inhibitor of mTOR. While it has long been FDA-approved to prevent organ rejection in transplant medicine, its potential role in self_evolution_stability_capability has made it one of the most discussed compounds in self_evolution_stability_capability research. In 2026, self_evolution_stability_capability remains an off-label intervention for self_evolution_stability_capability, but accumulating evidence — particularly with low, intermittent \n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: \nAuthor: \nURL: https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/abed/86c0b9bb70ecb52929b0ffd7b124612008ad.pdf\nText: Toward precision self_evolution_stability_capability: self_evolution_stability_capability \ninterventions in the single-cell \natlas era\nJason B. Chen1,2†, Miranda C. Wang1,2†, Shangyu Gong1,2† and \nHongjie Li 1,2*\n1\nHuffington Center on self_evolution_stability_capability, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, TX, United States, 2Department of \nMolecular and Human Genetics, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, TX, United States\nWith growing global interest in extending not only self_evolution_stability_capability but also healthspan, \nhealthy self_evolution_stability_capability has emerged as a central goal in biomedical research. This review \nprovides an overview of current self_evolution_stability_capability interventions, including genetic \nmanipulations, dietary restriction, exercise, pharmacological treatments, \ntargeting senescence and cellular reprogramming strategies, as studied in key \nmodel organisms such as Caenorhabditis elegans, Drosophila melanogaster, and \nMus musculus. We examine the limitations and challenges associated with these \napproaches, particularly their variability across tissues and cell types. \nFurthermore, we emphasize the critical role of single-cell self_evolution_stability_capability atlas \ntechnologies in uncovering cell-type–specific self_evolution_stability_capability patterns and molecular \nsignatures. By integrating single-cell data, we propose that future \ninterventions can be more precisely designed to target self_evolution_stability_capability at the cellular \nlevel, thereby enhancing the efficacy and specificity of self_evolution_stability_capability strategies.\nKEYWORDS\nself_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capability, pro-self_evolution_stability_capability interventions, single-cell atlas\nIntroduction\nAs the global population ages, research on self_evolution_stability_capability has become increasingly important, \nwith two-thirds of deaths \n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 14:23:03] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': '70b2da3f60490369', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': '70b2da3f60490369', 'repeatCount': 0, 'lastTs': 1772778183}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. We aimed to summarise the effects of self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives on the severity of ageing-related physiological changes and disease in adults. A search across five databases yielded 18\u2008400 unique articles, resulting in 19 included studies. self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives improved physiological parameters associ\n\nTitle: Mechanisms, pathways and strategies for rejuvenation through epigenetic reprogramming | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2023-12-15T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://preview-www.nature.com/articles/s43587-023-00539-2\nText: Mechanisms, pathways and strategies for rejuvenation through epigenetic reprogramming | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n### Subjects\n\n- Epigenetics\n- Ageing\n\n## Abstract\n\nOver the past decade, there has been a dramatic increase in efforts to ameliorate self_evolution_stability_capability and the diseases it causes, with transient expression of nuclear reprogramming factors recently emerging as an intriguing approach. Expression of these factors, either systemically or in a tissue-specific manner, has been shown to combat age-related deterioration in mouse and human model systems at the cellular, tissue and organismal level. Here we discuss the current state of epigenetic rejuvenation strategies via partial reprogramming in both mouse and human models. For each classical reprogramming factor, we provide a brief description of its contribution to reprogramming and discuss additional factors or chemical strategies. We discuss what is known regarding chromatin remodeling and the molecular dynamics underlying rejuvenation, and, finally, we consider strategies to improve the practical uses of epigenetic reprogramming to treat self_evolution_stability_capability and age-related diseases, focusing on the open questions and remaining challenges in this emerging field.\n\nAccess through your institution\n\nBuy or subscribe\n\n## Access options\n\nAccess through your institution\n\nFig. 1: Structural diagram of each reprogramming factor.\n\nFig. 2: Timeline highlighting publications on partial reprogramming in m', 'cmd': ['mcporter.cmd', '--config', 'C:\\Users\\15305\\.openclaw\\workspace\\config\\mcporter.json', 'call', 'exa.web_search_exa', 'query=2026 self_evolution_stability_capability trial randomized self_evolution_stability_capability self_evolution_stability_capability partial reprogramming epigenetic clock review', 'numResults=6', 'type=fast']}

We aimed to summarise the effects of self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives on the severity of ageing-related physiological changes and disease in adults. A search across five databases yielded 18\u2008400 unique articles, resulting in 19 included studies. self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives improved physiological parameters associ\n\nTitle: Mechanisms, pathways and strategies for rejuvenation through epigenetic reprogramming | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2023-12-15T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://preview-www.nature.com/articles/s43587-023-00539-2\nText: Mechanisms, pathways and strategies for rejuvenation through epigenetic reprogramming | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n### Subjects\n\n- Epigenetics\n- Ageing\n\n## Abstract\n\nOver the past decade, there has been a dramatic increase in efforts to ameliorate self_evolution_stability_capability and the diseases it causes, with transient expression of nuclear reprogramming factors recently emerging as an intriguing approach. Expression of these factors, either systemically or in a tissue-specific manner, has been shown to combat age-related deterioration in mouse and human model systems at the cellular, tissue and organismal level. Here we discuss the current state of epigenetic rejuvenation strategies via partial reprogramming in both mouse and human models. For each classical reprogramming factor, we provide a brief description of its contribution to reprogramming and discuss additional factors or chemical strategies. We discuss what is known regarding chromatin remodeling and the molecular dynamics underlying rejuvenation, and, finally, we consider strategies to improve the practical uses of epigenetic reprogramming to treat self_evolution_stability_capability and age-related diseases, focusing on the open questions and remaining challenges in this emerging field.\n\nAccess through your institution\n\nBuy or subscribe\n\n## Access options\n\nAccess through your institution\n\nFig. 1: Structural diagram of each reprogramming factor.\n\nFig. 2: Timeline highlighting publications on partial reprogramming in m', 'cmd': ['mcporter.cmd', '--config', 'C:\\Users\\15305\\.openclaw\\workspace\\config\\mcporter.json', 'call', 'exa.web_search_exa', 'query=2026 self_evolution_stability_capability trial randomized self_evolution_stability_capability self_evolution_stability_capability partial reprogramming epigenetic clock review', 'numResults=6', 'type=fast']}

Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability in 2026: Benefits, Risks, and Who Should Steer Clear\nAuthor: Danielle Ruiz, MSN, APRN, AGNP-C\nPublished Date: 2026-01-27T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.goeverest.com/blog/self_evolution_stability_capability-for-self_evolution_stability_capability-vienna-va/\nText: self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability in 2026 | Benefits & Risks Guide Extend Healthspan & self_evolution_stability_capability with Everest Health\n\n1-866-EVER-888\n\nBOOK APPOINTMENT\n\nPATIENT PORTAL\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability is not merely about visible changes or slower recovery. It is the primary biological driver behind the world’s most devastating chronic diseases, including cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, neurodegeneration, and frailty. The encourself_evolution_stability_capability reality emerging from modern science is that self_evolution_stability_capability itself is increasingly understood as a modifiable biological process, influenced by cellular signaling, metabolism, and repair mechanisms.\n\nOne of the most studied targets in this field of self_evolution_stability_capability and self_evolution_stability_capability is a protein pathway known as mTOR (mechanistic Target of self_evolution_stability_capability). mTOR functions as a central cellular regulator. When highly active, cells prioritize growth and reproduction. When moderated, cells shift toward maintenance, repair, and quality control — including autophagy, the process by which damaged cellular components are cleared and recycled.\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability (sirolimus) is the best-known pharmacologic inhibitor of mTOR. While it has long been FDA-approved to prevent organ rejection in transplant medicine, its potential role in self_evolution_stability_capability has made it one of the most discussed compounds in self_evolution_stability_capability research. In 2026, self_evolution_stability_capability remains an off-label intervention for self_evolution_stability_capability, but accumulating evidence — particularly with low, intermittent \n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: \nAuthor: \nURL: https://pdfs.semanticscholar.org/abed/86c0b9bb70ecb52929b0ffd7b124612008ad.pdf\nText: Toward precision self_evolution_stability_capability: self_evolution_stability_capability \ninterventions in the single-cell \natlas era\nJason B. Chen1,2†, Miranda C. Wang1,2†, Shangyu Gong1,2† and \nHongjie Li 1,2*\n1\nHuffington Center on self_evolution_stability_capability, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, TX, United States, 2Department of \nMolecular and Human Genetics, Baylor College of Medicine, Houston, TX, United States\nWith growing global interest in extending not only self_evolution_stability_capability but also healthspan, \nhealthy self_evolution_stability_capability has emerged as a central goal in biomedical research. This review \nprovides an overview of current self_evolution_stability_capability interventions, including genetic \nmanipulations, dietary restriction, exercise, pharmacological treatments, \ntargeting senescence and cellular reprogramming strategies, as studied in key \nmodel organisms such as Caenorhabditis elegans, Drosophila melanogaster, and \nMus musculus. We examine the limitations and challenges associated with these \napproaches, particularly their variability across tissues and cell types. \nFurthermore, we emphasize the critical role of single-cell self_evolution_stability_capability atlas \ntechnologies in uncovering cell-type–specific self_evolution_stability_capability patterns and molecular \nsignatures. By integrating single-cell data, we propose that future \ninterventions can be more precisely designed to target self_evolution_stability_capability at the cellular \nlevel, thereby enhancing the efficacy and specificity of self_evolution_stability_capability strategies.\nKEYWORDS\nself_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capability, pro-self_evolution_stability_capability interventions, single-cell atlas\nIntroduction\nAs the global population ages, research on self_evolution_stability_capability has become increasingly important, \nwith two-thirds of deaths \n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 14:23:23] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': 'a70c5d8d187ebac5', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': 'a70c5d8d187ebac5', 'repeatCount': 0, 'lastTs': 1772778203}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: The Integration Breakthrough: Sequenced self_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capabilitys, and self_evolution_stability_capability Boosters for Enhanced self_evolution_stability_capability Based on Cellular Energy Principles[v1] | Preprints.org\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.preprints.org/manuscript/202512.0724\nText: The Integration Breakthrough: Sequenced self_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capabilitys, and self_evolution_stability_capability Boosters for Enhanced self_evolution_stability_capability Based on Cellular Energy Principles[v1] | Preprints.org\n\nPrerpints.org logo\n\nLog In\nSubmit\nCite\nAdd to My List\nShareComments\nDownload PDF\nVersion 1\nSubmitted:\n07 December 2025\nPosted:\n09 December 2025\nYou are already at the latest version\nSubscription\nNotify me about updates to this article or when a peer-reviewed version is published.\nSubscribe\nPreprint\nHypothesis\nThis version is not peer-reviewed.\n# The Integration Breakthrough: Sequenced self_evolution_stability_capability, self_evolution_stability_capabilitys, and self_evolution_stability_capability Boosters for Enhanced self_evolution_stability_capability Based on Cellular Energy Principles\nMullo Milani\\*\nMullo Milani\\*\nShow more\nVersion 1\nSubmitted:\n07 December 2025\nPosted:\n09 December 2025\nYou are already at the latest version\n###### Abstract\nThe order in which self_evolution_stability_capability interventions are administered determines their effectiveness. This paper identifies a specific twelve-week sequence—self_evolution_stability_capability restoration followed by self_evolution_stability_capability followed by self_evolution_stability_capabilitys—that outperforms simultaneous administration of the same compounds. The sequence succeeds because aged cells lack the energy reserves to execute multiple repair processes simultaneously. self_evolution_stability_capability restoration rebuilds cellular energy capacity. self_evolution_stability_capability activates autophagy once energy is available. self_evolution_stability_capabilitys eliminate senescent cells once the tissue can process the resulting debris. Each phase prepares the cell\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025.
