- [2026-03-06 14:45:59] ACC={'ok': False, 'hash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'issues': [{'kind': 'repeating_payload', 'repeatCount': 12}], 'state': {'lastHash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'repeatCount': 12, 'lastTs': 1772779559}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. - [2026-03-06 14:46:19] ACC={'ok': False, 'hash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'issues': [{'kind': 'repeating_payload', 'repeatCount': 17}], 'state': {'lastHash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'repeatCount': 17, 'lastTs': 1772779579}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 14:46:28] ACC={'ok': False, 'hash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'issues': [{'kind': 'repeating_payload', 'repeatCount': 19}], 'state': {'lastHash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'repeatCount': 19, 'lastTs': 1772779588}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 14:46:36] ACC={'ok': False, 'hash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'issues': [{'kind': 'repeating_payload', 'repeatCount': 21}], 'state': {'lastHash': '9ae790a0879d3b33', 'repeatCount': 21, 'lastTs': 1772779596}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. We aimed to sum\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Mechanisms, pathways and strategies for rejuvenation through epigenetic reprogramming | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2023-12-15T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://preview-www.nature.com/articles/s43587-023-00539-2\nText: Mechanisms, pathways and strategies for rejuvenation through epigenetic reprogramming | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n### Subjects\n\n- Epigenetics\n- Ageing\n\n## Abstract\n\nOver the past decade, there has been a dramatic increase in efforts to ameliorate self_evolution_stability_capability and the diseases it causes, with transient expression of nuclear reprogramming factors recently emerging as an intriguing approach. Expression of these factors, either systemically or in a tissue-specific manner, has been shown to combat age-related deterioration in mouse and human model systems at the cellular, tissue and organismal level. Here we discuss the current state of epigenetic rejuvenation strategies via partial reprogramming in both mouse and human models. For each classical reprogramming factor, we provide a brief description of its contribution to reprogramming and discuss additional factors or chemical strategies. We discuss what is known regarding chromatin remodeling and the molecular dynamics underlying rejuvenation, and, finally, we consider strategies to improve the practical uses of epigenetic reprogramming to treat self_evolution_stability_capability and age-related diseases, focusing on the open questions and remaining challenges in this emerging field.\n\nAccess through your institution\n\nBuy or subscribe\n\n## Access options\n\nAccess through your institution\n\nFig. 1: Structural diagram of each reprogramming factor.\n\nFig. 2: Timeline highlighting publications on partial reprogramming in m', 'cmd': ['mcporter.cmd', '--config', 'C:\\Users\\15305\\.openclaw\\workspace\\config\\mcporter.json', 'call', 'exa.web_search_exa', 'query=2026 self_evolution_stability_capability  trial randomized self_evolution_stability_capability self_evolution_stability_capability partial reprogramming epigenetic clock review', 'numResults=6', 'type=fast']}

- [2026-03-06 14:53:31] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': 'f313c995bb9d4377', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': 'f313c995bb9d4377', 'repeatCount': 1, 'lastTs': 1772780011}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': 'Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Safety was assessed through advers\n\nTitle: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: Deborah J W Lee, MDa,† ∙ Ajla Hodzic Kuerec, MDa,b,† ∙ Prof Andrea B Maier, MDa,b,c a.b.maier@vu.nl\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.thelancet.com/journals/lanhl/article/PIIS2666-7568(23)00258-1/fulltext\nText: Skip to Main Content Skip to Main Menu\n\nADVERTISEMENT\n\nSCROLL TO CONTINUE WITH CONTENT\n\nOpen GPT ConsoleOpen Oracle KeywordsRefresh Values\n\n| Property | Value |\n| --- | --- |\n| Status |\n| Version |\n| Ad File |\n| Disable Ads Flag |\n| Environment |\n| Moat Init |\n| Moat Ready |\n| Contextual Ready |\n| Contextual URL |\n| Contextual Initial Segments |\n| Contextual Used Segments |\n| AdUnit |\n| SubAdUnit |\n| Custom Targeting |\n| Ad Events |\n| Invalid Ad Sizes |\n\nDownload PDFDownload PDF\n\nOutline\n\nShare\n\n#### Share\n\nShare on\n\n- Email\n- X\n- Facebook\n- LinkedIn\n- Sina Weibo\n- Mendeley\n- Bluesky\n\nAdd to my reading list\n\nMore\n\nMore\n\n- Download PDFDownload PDF\n- Cite\n- Share\n\n\n\n#### Share\n\n\n\nShare on\n\n- Email\n- X\n- Facebook\n- LinkedIn\n- Sina Weibo\n- Mendeley\n- Bluesky\n\nAdd to my reading list\n\n- Set Alert\n- Get Rights\n- Reprints\n\nDownload Full Issue\n\nDownload started\n\nOk\n\nPrevious articleNext article\n\n## Summary\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives (rapalogs) are inhibitors of mTOR, a major regulator of the ageing process. - [2026-03-06 16:45:50] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': 'be5f52e46b4eb0d5', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': 'be5f52e46b4eb0d5', 'repeatCount': 0, 'lastTs': 1772786750}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 5, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': "Title: The self_evolution_stability_capabilitys trametinib and self_evolution_stability_capability combine additively to extend mouse healthspan and self_evolution_stability_capability | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-05-28T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://preview-www.nature.com/articles/s43587-025-00876-4\nText: The self_evolution_stability_capabilitys trametinib and self_evolution_stability_capability combine additively to extend mouse healthspan and self_evolution_stability_capability | Nature self_evolution_stability_capability\n\nDownload PDF\n\n### Subjects\n\n- Mouse\n- Ageing\n\n## Abstract\n\nSuppression of the insulin–IGF–mTORC1–Ras network ameliorates self_evolution_stability_capability in animals. Many drugs have targets in the network because of its roles in cancer and metabolic disease and are candidates for repurposing as self_evolution_stability_capabilitys. self_evolution_stability_capability, an established geroprotective drug, blocks mTORC1 signaling, and trametinib inhibits the Ras–MEK–ERK pathway. In this study, we assessed survival and health of male and female mice treated with trametinib, self_evolution_stability_capability or their combination. We show here that trametinib treatment extended self_evolution_stability_capability in both sexes and that its combination with self_evolution_stability_capability was additive. Combination treatment reduced liver tumors in both sexes and spleen tumors in male mice, blocked the age-related increase in brain glucose uptake and strongly reduced inflammation in brain, kidney, spleen and muscle and circulating levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines. We conclude that trametinib is a self_evolution_stability_capability in mice and that its combination with self_evolution_stability_capability is more effective than either drug alone, making the combination a candidate for repurposing as a gerotherapy in humans.\n\n### Targeting the biology of self_evolution_stability_capability with mTOR inhibitors\n\n### Short term treatment with a cocktail of self_evolution_stability_capability, acarbose and phenylbutyrate delays self_evolution_stability_capability p\n\nTitle: Multi-omics characterization of partial chemical reprogramming reveals evidence of cell rejuvenation\nAuthor: Schumacher B.Pothof J.Vijg J.Hoeijmakers J. H. Department of Cell Biology, Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA 02115 United States\n3. For\narticles published under an open access Creative Common CC BY license, any part of the article may be reused without\npermission provided that the original article is cle\n\nTitle: Frontiers | The interplay of cellular senescence and reprogramming shapes the biological landscape of self_evolution_stability_capability and cancer revealing novel therapeutic avenues\nAuthor: Fuan Ding, Ying Yu, Jiangqi Zhao, Shibo Wei, Yan Zhang, Jung Ho Han, Zhuo Li, Hong-Bo Jiang, Dongryeol Ryu, Minkyoung Cho, Sung-Jin Bae, Wonyoung Park, Ki-Tae Ha, Bo Gao\nPublished Date: 2025-04-28T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/cell-and-developmental-biology/articles/10.3389/fcell.2025.1593096/full\nText: Frontiers | The interplay of cellular senescence and reprogramming shapes the biological landscape of self_evolution_stability_capability and cancer revealing novel therapeutic avenues\n**You're viewing our updated article page.** If you need more time to adjust, you can return to the old layout.\nSwitch to the old layout\n## REVIEW article\nFront. Cell Dev. Biol., 28 April 2025\nSec. Cell Growth and Division\nVolume 13 - 2025 | https://doi.org/10.3389/fcell.2025.1593096\n\nPublished in\nFrontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology\nFrontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology\n* #### Cell Growth and Division\n* **4.3** impact factor\n* **11.4** citescore\n\nPart of a Research Topic\nCellular Senescence in self_evolution_stability_capability-Related Diseases: Mechanisms and Therapeutic Targets\n* **34k** views\n* **10** articles\n\n### Editor &amp; Reviewers\nEdited by\n\nJZ\nJianjun Zhou\n\nReviewed by\n\nTC\nThomas Clarke\n\nRS\nRoshan Shrestha\n\nOutline\nFigures and Tables\n* FIGURE 1\nView in article\n* FIGURE 2\nView in article\n* FIGURE 3\nView in article\n* TABLE 1\nself_evolution_stability_capability agents targeting selective elimination of senescent cells.\nView in article\n* TABLE 2\nSenomorphic agents modulating the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP)\n\nTitle: Multi-omics characterization of partial chemical reprogramming reveals evidence of cell rejuvenation\nAuthor: \nURL: https://elifesciences.org/reviewed-preprints/90579\nText: Multi-omics characterization of partial chemical reprogramming reveals evidence of cell rejuvenation\n\neLife assessment\n\nThis important study reports comprehensive multi-omics data on the changes induced in young and aged male mouse tail fibroblasts after treatment with chemical reprogramming factors. The authors provide solid evidence to support their claim that chemical reprogramming factors induce changes consistent with a reduction of cellular 'biological' age (e.g., correlations with established self_evolution_stability_capability markers in whole tissues).\n\n- https://doi.org/10.7554/eLife.90579.2.sa2\n\n### Significance of findings\n\nimportant: Findings that have theoretical or practical implications beyond a single subfield\n\n- useful\n- valuable\n- important\n- fundamental\n- landmark\n\n### Strength of evidence\n\nsolid: Methods, data and analyses broadly support the claims with only minor weaknesses\n\n- inadequate\n- incomplete\n- solid\n- convincing\n- compelling\n- exceptional\n\nDuring the peer-review process the editor and reviewers write an eLife assessment that summarises the significance of the findings reported in the article (on a scale ranging from landmark to useful) and the strength of the evidence (on a scale ranging from exceptional to inadequate). Learn more about eLife assessments\n\n# Abstract\n\nPartial reprogramming by cyclic short-term expression of Yamanaka factors holds promise for shifting cells to younger states and consequently delaying the onset of many diseases of self_evolution_stability_capability. However\n\nTitle: Initiation phase cellular reprogramming ameliorates DNA damage in the ERCC1 mouse model of premature self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2024-01-23T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fragi.2023.1323194/full?field=&id=1323194&journalName=Frontiers_in_self_evolution_stability_capability\nText: Frontiers | Initiation phase cellular reprogramming ameliorates DNA damage in the ERCC1 mouse model of premature self_evolution_stability_capabilitySkip to main content\n* Download Article\nDownload Article\n* Download PDF\n* ReadCube\n* EPUB\n* XML (NLM)\n* Supplementary\nMaterial\n* Supplemental data\n* Share on\n* \n* \n* \n* Export citation\n* EndNote\n* Reference Manager\n* Simple TEXT file\n* BibTex\n* Total views\n* Downloads\n* Citations\nCitation numbers are available from Dimensions\nView article impact\n* View altmetric score\n##### SHARE ON\n* \n* \n* \n##### Edited by\n  ###### Olaya Santiago Fern&#225;ndez\n\nAlbert Einstein College of Medicine, United States\n##### Reviewed by\n  ###### Juan Antonio Fafian Labora\n\nUniversity of A Coru&#241;a, Spain\n  ###### Yuyao Tian\n\nMassachusetts General Hospital, Harvard Medical School, United States\n* Open supplemental data\n* Export citation\n* EndNote\n* Reference Manager\n* Simple TEXT file\n* BibTex\n\nCheck for updates\n##### People also looked at\n## ORIGINAL RESEARCH article\nFront. self_evolution_stability_capability, 23 January 2024\nSec. - [2026-03-06 16:55:19] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'repeatCount': 0, 'lastTs': 1772787319}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': "Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation.
