- [2026-03-06 16:57:12] ACC={'ok': True, 'hash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'issues': [], 'state': {'lastHash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'repeatCount': 1, 'lastTs': 1772787432}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': "Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. - [2026-03-06 16:57:33] ACC={'ok': False, 'hash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'issues': [{'kind': 'repeating_payload', 'repeatCount': 6}], 'state': {'lastHash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'repeatCount': 6, 'lastTs': 1772787453}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': "Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. - [2026-03-06 16:57:41] ACC={'ok': False, 'hash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'issues': [{'kind': 'repeating_payload', 'repeatCount': 8}], 'state': {'lastHash': 'ec9548789c08445a', 'repeatCount': 8, 'lastTs': 1772787461}} EVAL={'ok': True, 'evidence_strength': 10, 'novelty': 7, 'actionability': 7, 'risk': 9} FORAGE={'ok': True, 'returncode': 0, 'text': "Title: Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results | medRxiv\nAuthor: \nURL: https://www.medrxiv.org/content/10.1101/2024.08.21.24312372v1.full-text\nText: # Safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability on healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL Trial Results\n\n- Sajad Zalzala\n- Anar Isman\n- Stefanie L. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. Morgan\n- Jesper Hagemeier\n- Mauricio Moel\n- Andy Nyquist\n- Virginia Lee\n- Girish Harinath\n\n## Abstract\n\nself_evolution_stability_capability has been shown to have self_evolution_stability_capability-enhancing effects in murine models, but clinical data on its gerotherapeutic effects in humans remains limited. We performed a 48-week double-blinded, randomized, and placebo-controlled decentralized study (Participatory Evaluation of self_evolution_stability_capability with self_evolution_stability_capability for self_evolution_stability_capability [PEARL]; NCT04488601; registration date 2020-07-28) to evaluate the safety and efficacy of self_evolution_stability_capability in mitigating clinical signs of self_evolution_stability_capability in a normative self_evolution_stability_capability cohort. Participants received 5 or 10 mg / week of compounded self_evolution_stability_capability, or placebo for 48 weeks. Safety, adverse events (AEs) and blood biomarkers were collected. Efficacy was assessed using DEXA scan-based measures and standardized surveys assessing quality of life (QoL) and frailty. We did not detect significant differences in safety blood biomarkers, or moderate to severe AEs between the self_evolution_stability_capability treatment groups and placebo after 48 weeks. We detected dose-dependent (10 mg group) and sex-specific improvements in lean tissue mass, pain, social functioning, overall QoL, and overall osteoarthritis score in females, and in bone mineral content in men. Additionally, some individuals receiving self_evolution_stability_capability experienced significant improvements \n\nTitle: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\nAuthor: Nadia Tresoro\nPublished Date: 2026-02-12T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://wellnessforum.pro/en/er-100-the-first-clinical-trial-of-partial-epigenetic-reprogramming-in-humans/\nText: ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\nNews, Gurus, self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# ER-100: the first clinical trial of partial epigenetic reprogramming in humans\n\n###### Published on\n\n- 12 February, 2026\n\n- Nadia Tresoro\n\nFor decades we assumed that ageing was a linear, cumulative and inevitable process. Today, biology is challenging that paradigm. Epigenetics - the system of chemical marks that regulates which genes are turned on or silenced - has become one of the most promising territories in self_evolution_stability_capability medicine.\n\nWhat once seemed like science fiction is now entering the clinical phase: partial epigenetic reprogramming already being tested in humans.\n\n## The origin of the concept: resetting without erasing identity\n\nCell reprogramming became world-renowned with the discovery of Yamanaka's factors (OCT4, SOX2, KLF4 and c-MYC), which are capable of converting adult cells into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). This process erases cell identity and resets the biological state to an embryonic stage.\n\nThe problem: complete reprogramming implies loss of specialised function and risk of tumourigenicity.\n\nThe solution that has emerged in the last decade is the partial reprogrammingTemporary activation of some factors (usually OCT4, SOX2 and KLF4, without c-MYC) to rejuvenate the epigenome without de-differentiating the cell.\n\nIn animal models, this str\n\nTitle: self_evolution_stability_capability Treatments in Human Trials (2024–2025)\nAuthor: \nPublished Date: 2025-07-02T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.p05.org/self_evolution_stability_capability-treatments-in-human-trials-2024-2025/\nText: The field of self_evolution_stability_capability therapeutics has rapidly progressed from animal studies to **human clinical trials** in recent years. Multiple approaches – from self_evolution_stability_capabilitys to gene therapy – are being tested in clinics around the world. **Figure 1** illustrates the geographic span of these trials (primarily in the US, with some in Europe, Asia, and South America), and **Figure 2** shows the variety of therapeutic _targets_ under investigation. The sections below detail each therapy, linking to sources (including clinical trial identifiers where available), current trial phases, expected readouts, participant numbers, and key findings to date.\n\n_Figure 1: Global locations of major self_evolution_stability_capability trials in 2024–2025. Red dots indicate trial sites (USA has multiple clusters; EU planned sites; trial in Singapore; a paid gene therapy in Colombia) based on sources_ _afar.org_ _clinicaltrialsarena.com_ _._\n\n_Figure 2: Frequency of therapies by target mechanism in current human trials. self_evolution_stability_capability and NAD-boosting approaches dominate, followed by metabolic modulators, plasma-based therapies, etc., as derived from the list below._\n\n## self_evolution_stability_capability Therapies (Clearing “Zombie” Cells)\n\n### **Dasatinib + Quercetin (D+Q) – _self_evolution_stability_capability combo (senescent cell clearance), Phase\xa02 completed._**\n\nThis combination of a cancer drug (dasatinib) and a flavonoid (quercetin) was the first self_evolution_stability_capability tested in humans. In 2024, Mayo Clinic researchers published a 20-week **Phase\xa02 RCT** in 60 women (age\n\nTitle: Review Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review\nAuthor: GBD 2019 Demographics Collaborators\nPublished Date: 2024-02-01T00:00:00.000Z\nURL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666756823002581\nText: Targeting ageing with self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives in humans: a systematic review - ScienceDirect\nSkip to main contentSkip to article\nElsevier logoScienceDirect\nMy account\nSign in\n* View**PDF**\n* Download full issue\nSearch ScienceDirect\n## Outline\n1. We aimed to summarise the effects of self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives on the severity of ageing-related physiological changes and disease in adults. A search across five databases yielded 18\u2008400 unique articles, resulting in 19 included studies. self_evolution_stability_capability and its derivatives improved physiological parameters associ\n\nTitle: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\nAuthor: Mauricio Moel 1 , 2 , \n        \n         \n       ,\nURL: https://www.self_evolution_stability_capability-us.com/article/206235/text\nText: Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results | self_evolution_stability_capability\n\n# Influence of self_evolution_stability_capability on safety and healthspan metrics after one year: PEARL trial results\n\n#### Mauricio Moel1,2, , Girish Harinath1,2, , Virginia Lee1,2, , Andy Nyquist1, , Stefanie L. Morgan1,2, , Anar Isman1, , Sajad Zalzala1, ,\n\n- 2 Division of Research and Applied Sciences, AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n- 1 AgelessRx, Ann Arbor, MI 48104, USA\n\n#### Received: December 2, 2024 Accepted: March 25, 2025 Published: April 4, 2025\n\nhttps://doi.org/10.18632/self_evolution_stability_capability.206235 How to Cite\n\nCopyright: © 2025 Moel et al. This is an open access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License(CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original author and source are credited.\n\n### Abstract\n\nDesign: This 48-week decentralized, double-blinded, randomized, placebo-controlled trial (NCT04488601) evaluated the long-term safety of intermittent low-dose self_evolution_stability_capability in a healthy, normative-self_evolution_stability_capability human cohort. Participants received placebo, 5 mg or 10 mg compounded self_evolution_stability_capability weekly. The primary outcome measure was visceral adiposity (by DXA scan), secondary outcomes were blood biomarkers, and lean tissue and bone mineral content (by DXA scan). Established surveys were utilized to evaluate health and well-being.
